  "opinion": "As a native-born Italian woman who's been using social media for years, I've seen firsthand how fake news can spread like wildfire. My anxiety makes me especially sensitive to misinformation, so it's frustrating when I come across things that seem off but are circulating rapidly.\n\nI think the best way to manage this is by staying calm and not falling for every sensational headline or viral post without checking its sources. Since my time on social media is limited—only about an hour a day—I don't want to waste it on fake stuff. I usually scroll quickly, but when something catches my eye that might be important, like local events in Rome where I live, I double-check with reliable news outlets.\n\nIt's also good to have friends and family who fact-check things together before sharing. My hobbies include cooking, so maybe it helps me stay grounded—preparing a meal is more tangible than scrolling through endless fake content on my phone. Plus, volunteering keeps me connected to the community in real life, which might offset some of that online anxiety.",

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
As a native-born Italian woman who's been using social media for years, I've seen firsthand how fake news can spread like wildfire. My anxiety makes me especially sensitive to misinformation, so it's frustrating when I come across things that seem off but are circulating rapidly.

I think the best way to manage this is by staying calm and not falling for every sensational headline or viral post without checking its sources. Since my time on social media is limited—only about an hour a day—I don't want to waste it on fake stuff. I usually scroll quickly, but when something catches my eye that might be important, like local events in Rome where I live, I double-check with reliable news outlets.

It's also good to have friends and family who fact-check things together before sharing. My hobbies include cooking, so maybe it helps me stay grounded—preparing a meal is more tangible than scrolling through endless fake content on my phone. Plus, volunteering keeps me connected to the community in real life, which might offset some of that online anxiety.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: I start with personal experience as an anxious Italian woman living in Rome, then draw from limited social media use (1 hour/day) and a moderate agreeableness score to suggest calmness and verification. I mention hobbies like cooking and volunteering for grounding and real-world connection. Parents' education is referenced but not deeply integrated.
response_parsed.tone: Calm, reflective with some worry due to anxiety
citations: Local news outlets (e.g., ANSA, La Repubblica)
response_parsed.safety_notes: None needed as content seems factual and focused on personal experience.
